name: UserInfo endpoint
outcome: A relying party can present an access token to a UserInfo endpoint and receive the end-user's scope-derived claims, proven end-to-end by the keystone test.
scope: Add the UserInfo handler and the AuthorizationServer.userInfo() method, plus the keystone e2e covering the full OIDC loop.
acceptance_criteria: UserInfo returns { sub, ...scope-derived claims } as JSON for a valid openid-scoped access token
A token without the openid scope yields 403 insufficient_scope
An invalid or expired token yields 401 invalid_token with a WWW-Authenticate header
The keystone e2e passes: authorize, token, verify id_token against live /jwks, then /userinfo
steps: src/oidc — add a UserInfo handler that extracts the bearer access token (Authorization header and form/query), verifies signature and expiry, and checks revocation when getByAccessToken is implemented
UserInfo handler — return 403 insufficient_scope when the token lacks the openid scope, and 401 invalid_token with a WWW-Authenticate header for bad tokens
UserInfo handler — call getUserClaims with the user identifier, apply the scope filter, and return { sub, ...claims } as JSON
AuthorizationServer — add a userInfo(req) method delegating to the handler

test/e2e — keystone test on the vanilla adapter: authorize with scope=openid plus nonce plus PKCE, exchange the code, verify the id_token against the live /jwks endpoint, then call /userinfo and assert claims
notes: getUserClaims is keyed by userId, not a loaded user object, so no user repository is needed in the OIDC config. UserInfo returns plain JSON only in v1. The keystone e2e is the highest-value test — it proves the key plumbing, not just claim shapes.
